speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not-- not allowed to, you know, to read?

speaker: interviewer
text: Nope. That's not my rule. It's Mr. Spielberg's rule. I'm going to have to move just this light [INAUDIBLE]. Do you think I should move the water down on the coffee table so it will be more reachable for him?

speaker: interviewer
text: The what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The water. The water and the tissues.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I can move them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Today is January 18, 1996. We are conducting an interview with Henry Weil, H-E-N-R-Y W-E-I-L. The interviewer is Susan Rosenblum. S-U-S-A-N R-O-S-E-N-B-L-U-M. We are in Sunrise, Florida, United States of America, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today is January 18, 1996. We are conducting an interview with Henry Weil, W-E-I-L. I am Susan Rosenblum, R-O-S-E-N-B-L-U-M. We are in Sunrise, Florida, the United States of America. The language is English, and this is tape one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry Weil, W-E-I-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Olkusz, O-L-K-U-S-Z , Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born September the 10th, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today I am 81.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name-- Solomon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother? [? HW: ?] Jadwiga. Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My brother's name is Wolf. And my sisters-- Andzir and my-- and the other sister, Lola.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home like when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still young, and I was-- I was 18 years old. We were-- becase-- before the war, I was working in a bank, Bank [? Kupietzky, ?] for seven years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What large city was your hometown near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our hometown-- the hometown was [INAUDIBLE] in Olkusz. We moved to Mirkow where my father used to live, and then a couple years later, we moved to Cracow. In Cracow, we were till the end till-- till 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shabbat like in you home? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How did you-- what was it like to observe Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat-- we were-- my father was a religious man. In the same house where we used to live, you know, in Cracow, was a room in the same building, and my father has everything. The service, has-- you know, he did, you know. He, you know-- he did the Torah and everything, you know, all the time, you know. And then only when the Germans went-- they came in to Cracow, you know, was-- was quiet, and only on the Shabbat. And before the war, it was everyday, in the morning-- in the morning and the evening, also, every week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother make special foods for Sabbath?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, was-- she made [INAUDIBLE] and fish, you know? My mother was a real, you know, Jewish woman, and a sweet one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went only to-- to public school. Of course, I didn't have a chance to go higher because in the small town in Mirkow, you know, that's, you know, was too-- no one went, you know, to a high school, only public school. And then we moved away to Cracow. And in Cracow, my father has a store, a grocery store. And I helped him, also, in this store.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the town you lived in before you move to Cracow, what was the Jewish community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very-- very-- the Jewish community was very nice, you know. Almost everybody we know. And-- and that's, of course, like life is supposed to be, like-- like a family, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the Jewish community, do you know? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not too-- was maybe about 500 Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: But Cracow is a large city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- did you have any hobbies that you enjoyed doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was a skate-- I-- I-- I liked to skate. And I was skating on ice a couple years in Poland. Then when we came to United States, I was skating. And I teach, you know, my-- my and my daughter skating, also in the Rockefeller Center.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like for you when you moved to Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I moved to Cracow, I-- I help my father in the store till I was 18 years old. And then I-- I went to the Polish army. And I was in the Polish army one and a half years from 1938 till 1939, just a few weeks before Hitler came to Poland, the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the army like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The army was not bad, because in-- I-- I was in the army in Cracow, and we lived in Cracow. The six months, you know, I couldn't go home for a couple hours with the street train. But after, I could go everyday home for a couple hours from 7:00 to 11 o'clock. As long, to be back, you know, for at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother and sisters move to Cracow with you and your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother moved to Cracow, and my-- and my little sister. And my other sister, she got married, and she moved to Stanislawow. That's in-- in Poland. And then-- then-- then Hitler came in. They said, you know, that it's impossible, you know, that's-- right away they will take men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- my brother-in-law, he had a chance. He-- he ran away to Russia. But-- and my sister was with a small baby, she was still in Stanislawow, because then we were in the ghetto, you know, when, you know-- My father tried to get somebody-- a Pollack-- a Polish man. And he brought her home, you know, to be with us. Did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And-- when we had the store-- when he had the store, you know, I was at the time in a uniform-- in a Polish uniform. They came-- one Pollack came at night, you know, and he broke the window from the-- from the-- from the basement. And he went into the store, and he wanted to take out everything. But-- and this-- this night, you know, I was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was home, you know? I went to him, you know, and I gave him a couple of times-- that he will remember forever. You know, I wanted to kill him, because, for sure, in the cold, I will win. Because he tried, you know, to-- the uniform, the uniform to take it, you know, to rip off from me, you know? And that's not allowed. And that's [INAUDIBLE]. The president, Marshal Pilsudski, was that time, and he was not an anti-Semite. When Hitler came to power in 1933--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did that affect your life in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: As long as he was in Germany, it was OK. Because as long, you know, in Poland, it was OK. But 1930-- when he came in 1939, that was exactly September the 1st, 1939. He came. The first-- the first city was Cracow. That-- that was very bad. Took away all the stores, all the homes and everything, you know? And he started right away from the beginning. And he said to the Germans, you know, now you will have plenty homes from the Jews and plenty businesses and everything. And they-- and he start-- he start sending-- you have more-- more people, more-- more-- more Germans who want to go with Hitler. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- when-- when Hitler-- when Germany came into Poland in 1939, and they came to Cracow, where did you go? And your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he came to Cracow and was here-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oskar Schindler came up, because I have a cousin. You know, I had a cousin. His name was Abe Banker. He has a factory, Emalia. And he found out. Oskar Schindler found out, you know, that we had the keys from the factory. He came to us because my brother was the manager before the war in the factory. So the keys were there. And this was my cousin. When he-- he came, he ate with us lunch-- Oskar Schindler. And he told me stories [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] told-- that he is not a Nazi. He is only in the party. He want-- he want-- he doesn't care about the business, but he wants to show the world that he can help, you know, at least between 900 and 1,000 people-- that's what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the stories he told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me, you know, that any time-- before he left, you know, first of all, before he left he gave my mother a kiss and my little sister a kiss. And he-- I showed him to the door, and he said to me, Heinrich-- Henry is Heinrich-- any time, if you will be in Cracow-- because I told him that we are going to move to Wolbrom to a small town. Anytime you will be in Cracow, in Plaszow, whatever is-- you know, in ghetto, try to see me. Try to see me, because, you know, and over there, I will try the best for you. And I follow-- that's what he told me, and he left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: About that he was very good, you know? I remember, you know, that I was, you know, a policeman, you know. But when I came back, you know, from-- that's a further burden. When I came back-- when I came back, you know, to Plaszow, to the ghetto, and I was in-- in his-- in his place, you know, people-- the Jewish people were working very hard in the factory. But he-- I got the best job, you know, in-- in the place over there. And he gave me a lot of money, and a bunker, too, that's to give to the Pollacks. And they will bring to you, everything that you wanted. And he gave me a special place, you know, not a double bed, but he gave me a window that I will have more air and everything to be comfortable. He was, to me, very nice. He was to everybody nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you had been in Cracow? Your-- you said your family moved. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Cracow. We heard rumors that it's-- it's no good to be in a big city, so we moved-- my parents-- and we all moved, you know, to Wolbrom. In Wolbrom. It's a small town about 50 miles from Cracow. And over there, we came, you know, because my-- my father has a sister in Wolbrom. When we came over there, we went straight to the ghetto. And over there in the ghetto, we were two years, till 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you able to take with you into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just-- just a few things. Just a few things, you know. Just a few things that we-- we could get in the hands. Small suitcases.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my mother, and my two-- my-- my-- one-- the little sister. And I was there. Because my-- the other sister was in Stanislawow-- still in Stanislawow. From Wolbrom, we-- my father tried to get, you know-- you know, the-- my-- my-- the other sister who was married, you know, to bring her so she would be able to be with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was in Rzeszow, also in Poland, because he was married. You know, and he was in-- in a-- in a ghetto in Rzeszow with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the ghetto like in Wolbrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wolbrom, a couple of streets-- three, four streets, and that's-- that's it, you know? And we had a small-- we had-- we had one room. You can imagine, you know. Before, we had five rooms, now we had-- and over there in Wolbrom. But we were happy. At least we are together. Whatever will be, we'll be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto closed in by a wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not by a wall, but, you know, but they were-- they were watching, you know-- the SS was watching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to work while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food and clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could get it, you know. Not clothing, you know, because we took some from home, you know, in the suitcase. But my father's sister was living a long time in Wolbrom, so she helped us. She helped us with food, you know. She came every day, every second day. [INAUDIBLE] next block. And she brought us. We didn't eat so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like, or the sanitary conditions, in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too good, you know? We couldn't take every day a shower. You know, a lot of things was no good, becasue-- but we were happy that we still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there schools for the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In ghetto? No. No, no, no schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any synagogues?

speaker: interviewee
text: Synagogues, you know, private. Some-- sometimes, you know, the-- the-- the-- the service was in our-- in our room-- in our-- where we used to live, you know, it was a big room, you know. How long it took-- a half an hour. We were afraid. We were watching, you know, from the window, you know, oh, SS men doesn't look in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to make time pass quickly? What did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special. Nothing special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any selections made from this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The selection, they made in 1942. In 19-- made-- they liquidated the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- when did you go into-- when did you go into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't exactly remember, but we were there close to two years. In 1942, first-- first the liquidation. The ghetto was-- there's a liquidation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- the older people will not be able to go, so it was prepared, you know. The [INAUDIBLE] prepared, you know, horses with-- with wagons. And-- and there-- and there-- you know, they went over there. At first, it was 450. And between were my-- my father, also, because he was-- he was not able to go. He was-- he had only one leg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he went over there, you know, was-- was already prepared a grave. A mass-- a mass grave. And all of them are dead. They sprayed, you know, with some-- some [INAUDIBLE] or something like that, you know what I mean-- that's not too feeling-- you know, that's-- in the dirt on the top. So they took your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and myself and my sister-- one sister-- you know, my two sisters, because the one from Stanislawow. She was already in Wolbrom, too, with her baby. We went, you know, to the railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see your father be killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the policeman-- the Jewish policeman-- who was in ghetto, you know, came over. Because they cleaned up the ghetto, so he came over. You could go-- go. So he said, you see it, Henry. You know what? Your father is dead. I found, you know, his [INAUDIBLE], his [? select. ?] He-- but he came and he told me that. And-- and then-- then in the same day, was liquidation, you know, the whole ghetto. They took, you know, all the rest people. My mother, my two sisters, myself, and the little baby, they took, you know, to the railroad station. Over there was a-- we were over there a nice couple hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And when we came over there, with us first, maybe about-- about 3,000 without food, without water. When we came over there, wagons were already prepared. And-- and so just the young men they took out, you know, from the group. And they sent-- they send-- and they sent us away to [INAUDIBLE]-- to-- to Stalowa Wola. Stalowa Wola-- that's in Poland, a steel factory. And all the rest, you know, went into the black wagons, you know, and send to Chelmno. To Chelmno, to death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we-- I came to-- to Stalowa Wola.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the trip to the factory? It was many-- a day, about 24 hours. Went very slow, very slow. And you had no food or water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No food and not water. Because when we came over there, when we came to Stalowa Wola-- was-- not only from Wolbrom, you know, but it was from other-- other cities, also. From small cities was also there. Was all together, maybe between 9,000 and 10,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: No-- no beds, nothing. You know. When we came over there, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the-- we-- they show-- it was at night. They show us, you know, a big room where we will sleep, you know, on the floor. It was cold. It was winter. And a thousand people on the floor, you know, to spread the feet in between here, like the headings. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS men, every night-- he walked like-- like he walked on a carpet-- killed about 10, 15 people. When we get up in the morning, about 10, 15 people were dead. That's what happened every night.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the morning, the same thing when we were standing, you know, to go to work, 5 o'clock in the morning. We got just-- when we were standing in the line to get breakfast-- was hot boiled water. Hot boiled water. No sugar, no tea, no coffee, nothing. Just to hold the hands like that. Boiled water. They can die right away holding like that. If somebody didn't want to hold their hand like that, right away he killed. You know? So I said, you know, in a short time, so many people, you know, disappeared in the forest. The camp-- the camp was around the forest. What kind of work did you do in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean in Stalowa Wola.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stalowa Wola was a factory. We-- we walked five miles-- five kilometers-- every day, each way. When we came over there, we worked, you know, like everybody else. You know. It-- it-- it very-- labor-- labor work. Labor work in the factory. You know. They-- they-- and they made steel. You know, it was a very hard work, you know? Sunday, one Sunday when we were not working, they gave us a soup. And the soup was just plain water. How I-- I survived so many weeks and months, I-- it's-- I can't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you special clothes to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Like-- like they-- like in-- in jail, you know, with the stripes and a number. A number here, and a number on the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, you know, by the end, the American came and they found me whenever-- I'd been around. I had run away. We will [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody that was in the factory with you from your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the hometown? Yes, my cousin. My-- you know-- and he was-- he probably-- two days later, they shot him. A few days later. His name was Otto Weil. Was a very close cousin. We used to live in the same house, the same house, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you would come in at the end of the day, what would you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were talking, you know, [INAUDIBLE] that everybody said the same thing. Looks like we are next. How-- how we-- we could see, you know, with our own eyes what a sight-- can-- can do it. You know, how is he a miserable? You know? Once he said, you know, when he killed the people, he said, I would like to see how Jewish blood is running like water. That's what he said. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the SS man. And I-- I don't want to say, but he-- he was a miserable. He was some anti-Semite.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a tape break now.
